era_context: The Late Bronze Age international system was at its height, with Egypt under Amenhotep II, the Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I, and Mycenaean Greece as dominant powers. This period is characterized by extensive diplomatic correspondence, trade networks, and cultural exchange. The Amarna Letters archive documents international relations during this era. In China, the Shang Dynasty was in its mature phase. This was a period of relative stability and prosperity in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Amenhotep II's reign at its height
events.description: Amenhotep II's reign was at its height, with Egypt at the peak of its imperial power. The pharaoh maintained tight control over the military, priesthood, and administrative apparatus. Temple construction and religious patronage continued. Egypt's wealth and power were derived from control of Nubian gold, Levantine trade, and agricultural production in the Nile Valley.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and administrative records

events.certainty_note: General pattern of Amenhotep II's reign is well-documented; specific events of this year are less clearly attested.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's peak of power in the Near East
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I was at the height of his power, having consolidated Hittite control over much of Anatolia and northern Syria. The Hittite Empire was a major Near Eastern power, rivaling Egypt in influence. The Hittite state maintained sophisticated administrative and military structures. Diplomatic correspondence with Egypt and other powers was extensive.
events.certainty_note: General pattern is well-documented; specific events of this year are less clearly attested.

events.title: Mycenaean civilization at its peak
events.description: Mycenaean civilization was at its peak, with palace centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos functioning as sophisticated administrative and religious hubs. Linear B script was used for administrative record-keeping. Extensive trade networks connected the Mycenaean world to Egypt, the Levant, and Anatolia. Palatial architecture, pottery, and religious practices show a highly organized society.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean palace sites

events.certainty_note: Extensive archaeological evidence for Mycenaean civilization during this period.

events.title: International trade networks in the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Extensive trade networks connected Egypt, the Hittite Empire, Mycenaean Greece, and the Levant. Goods exchanged included tin, copper, cedar, olive oil, wine, pottery, and luxury items. These trade networks were crucial to the economies of all major powers and facilitated cultural exchange. The Amarna Letters document trade agreements and disputes.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of trade goods distribution

events.certainty_note: Extensive archaeological and documentary evidence for trade networks during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Amenhotep II's reign and specific events within it remains uncertain; some scholars place major events earlier or later. The extent of Hittite control in Syria is debated, with some scholars arguing that Egyptian influence remained stronger than Hittite sources suggest. The significance of Mycenaean trade may be overstated in modern scholarship relative to other economic systems. The Amarna Letters are difficult to date precisely to individual years, and some scholars debate whether all correspondence attributed to this period actually belongs to it.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1356 BCE is relatively rich compared to earlier periods, with Egyptian temple inscriptions, Hittite archives, the Amarna Letters, and extensive archaeological evidence. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and economic history. The perspectives of non-elite populations, women, and non-state societies are largely absent from written records. The Amarna Letters provide valuable diplomatic information but are difficult to date precisely to individual years. Chronological uncertainty remains ±2-3 years. The record is also biased toward the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East; other regions are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Egyptian and Hittite powers were at their height simultaneously, creating a bipolar international system.
Mycenaean civilization participated in the broader international trade networks of the Eastern Mediterranean.
International trade networks were crucial to Egyptian imperial power and wealth.
International trade networks were crucial to Hittite imperial power and wealth.
